Ey is currently one of the most popular neopronouns out there but it is in a decline in the language and has been for some time because it is perceived as being irregular.

Pronouns order
They (dē, dēm, deē, deēs, deēseēf)
Xe (zē, zēm, zeē, zeēs, zeēseēf)
Ey (ē, ēm, eē, eēs, eēseēf)
Fae (fē, fēm, feē, feēs, feēseēf)
Vi (vē, vēm, veē, veēs, veēseēf)
Ne (nē, nēm, neē, neēs, neēseēf)
Per (pē, pēm, peē, peēs, peēseēf)
Hu* (hē, hēm, heē, heēs, heēseēf)
She* (shē, shēm, sheē, sheēs, sheēseēf)

This is roughly what the order of nepronoun usage in English is.

neutral which does not modify the tone of the final syllable, and final syllables of words are always high tone or low tone,

Non-dropped 1st person noun types:

1. "My-type nouns"
Ones that use the inalienable possessive to describe something like a body part. My body, my name, my soul, my ass. These ones tend to be used to express nuances of the action happening

1.5. "1p->3p nouns"
"My-type nouns" that are declined in the third person and used for specific grammatical contexts. Mainly jussive and optative. You cannot use your name in a sentence like this, it is considered ungrammatical and would imply someone else with the same name. The possessive makes it clear that you are changing person.

2. "Social nouns"
These ones tend to be used to imply a relationship between the speaker and other people in the conversation. The "me and you" noun is a common one. These are all plural, and usually dyadic. Their pluralization works a bit weirdly 

3. "Common pronominalized nouns"
These ones are just common nouns or proper nouns that are declined in 1st person. This tends to be done in official contexts. A police officer might refer to themselves as "officer.1p.SING.MALE". Some people use their names like this, usually as a way of introducing themselves.

Pronominalized nouns are extremely common in the language, and there are no true pronouns.

About mine
Subordinate clauses are almost always SVO
Okay so VSO is the casual word order

SVO and VSO are the most common word orders. Generally speaking people will say say that SVO is the more common one, but this just isn’t really true. People tend to assume that a phrase with a dropped subject would be realized as SVO if the subject was not dropped. SVO is considered to be the most unambiguous word order. Because it clearly separates the subject and object by many syllables. But the reality of the situation is that VSO Word order is actually more common. If the subject and object are not of the same gender or number or person, but are both not dropped then they tend to be put in a VSO order. Although active verbs are more resistant to this.
All of this stuff is things that many native speakers violate but it is considered to be the most clear way of speaking. In school, you will be taught to do this thing.

SVO word order for transitive
SV for active
VS for stative 

The sentence that I gave followed the standard stative word order only in the second stative clause

The reason for this is that when a subordinate clause has a subject which is possessed by the subject of the superclause (not necessarily independent) then the possessed noun must come first in the clause. The word order is generally SVO but this is a situation where it is traditionally considered mandatory to do SVO.

This happening with two inanimate nouns is something where it is clear from the word order and the main reason why you are supposed to use the particle is because if part of the next clause is lost then it is very unclear what the subject of the second clause is

more of a linguistic prescriptivism thing really but the rule is that if you ever have two clauses with the same subject that that has the same same same person/gender markers you must use the grammatical particle Nono to indicate a switch in the subject
